narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 99.2% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your academic decile in Year 3 of school, where your ranking in the 2nd decile stood out as a major push toward predicting failure—far below the typical 6th decile average among students who passed the bar. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also at the 2nd level compared to passers' average around the 6th, which further tilted the prediction against you by signaling early struggles that often persist. Your upper-middle family income bracket played a smaller but still notable role in nudging the prediction toward failure, as it didn't align as strongly with the profiles of successful bar takers as lower or middle incomes sometimes do in the data. These three factors together overwhelmed any positive signals from your full-time enrollment plans or other details like your LSAT score of 34 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90, both of which fell short of the 37+ LSAT and 3.24 GPA averages for those who passed. In contrast, admitted students typically show much stronger early and later academic rankings, often in the top half of their classes, alongside balanced test scores and GPAs that predict bar success. While your background as a full-time Black female applicant matches many who succeed, the model focused purely on these performance indicators without considering gender or race. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's a data-driven flag about bar exam risks tied to your grades. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim for 37 or higher, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to boost those deciles, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools prioritizing bar passage. Many students in similar spots have rebounded stronger—your next steps can rewrite this story.
